title: F-Strings and Formatting
subtitle: Embed variables and expressions directly inside strings the modern Python way
learning_objectives: Create f-strings using the f"..." syntax to embed variables in text
Place any valid Python expression inside the curly braces of an f-string
Format numbers to a fixed number of decimal places using f"{value:.2f}"
Use the !r and !s conversion flags to control how values are displayed

sections.heading: F-String Basics and Expressions
sections.content:
An f-string (formatted string literal) lets you embed variables and expressions directly inside a string. Just put an f (or F) immediately before the opening quote, then place anything you want evaluated inside curly braces {}.

Before f-strings were added in Python 3.6, developers had to use % formatting or .format(), which were harder to read. F-strings are now the recommended approach because the variable name sits right where it appears in the output.

You can put any valid Python expression inside the braces — not just variable names. Arithmetic, method calls, comparisons, function calls — all are fair game. Python evaluates the expression first and then converts the result to a string.
sections.code_example:
# Basic variable embedding
name = "Alice"
age = 30
print(f"Hello, {name}! You are {age} years old.")

# Expressions inside {}
print(f"2 + 2 = {2 + 2}")
print(f"Name in uppercase: {name.upper()}")
print(f"Age in 10 years: {age + 10}")

# Multi-variable expression
width = 5
height = 3
print(f"A {width} by {height} rectangle has area {width * height}.")
sections.code_output:
Hello, Alice! You are 30 years old.
2 + 2 = 4
Name in uppercase: ALICE
Age in 10 years: 40
A 5 by 3 rectangle has area 15.

sections.heading: Number Formatting and Conversion Flags
sections.content:
Inside the curly braces, you can add a format spec after a colon (:) to control exactly how a value is displayed — without changing the underlying variable.

:.2f formats a number as a float with exactly 2 decimal places. The f stands for fixed-point. You can change 2 to any number of decimal places you need.

:, adds a thousands separator (a comma) to large numbers, making them easier to read.

The conversion flags !r and !s go before the colon (or replace it). !r calls repr() on the value, which shows the programmer-facing representation — strings get their quote marks, escape characters are shown literally. !s calls str(), which is what you normally see. !r is especially useful for debugging because it makes invisible characters visible.
sections.code_example:
price = 19.5
print(f"Price: ${price:.2f}")
print(f"Pi is approximately {3.14159:.4f}")

big_number = 1000000
print(f"Population: {big_number:,}")

# !s and !r conversion flags
value = "hello\nworld"
print(f"With !s: {value!s}")   # normal str() — newline is printed
print(f"With !r: {value!r}")   # repr() — shows \n literally

# Useful for debugging
user_input = "  Alice  "
print(f"Raw input is: {user_input!r}")
sections.code_output:
Price: $19.50
Pi is approximately 3.1416
Population: 1,000,000
With !s: hello
world
With !r: 'hello\nworld'
Raw input is: '  Alice  '

exercises.title: Personal Introduction
exercises.prompt: Create variables `name = "Sam"` and `year = 2024`. Use an f-string to print the sentence: `My name is Sam and it is 2024.`
exercises.starter_code:
name = "Sam"
year = 2024

# Use an f-string to print: My name is Sam and it is 2024.
exercises.solution:
name = "Sam"
year = 2024
print(f"My name is {name} and it is {year}.")
hints: Put f before the opening quote: f"..."
Place variables inside curly braces where they should appear: {name} and {year}

exercises.title: Format a Price
exercises.prompt: Given `price = 7.5` and `item = "coffee"`, use an f-string to print: `A coffee costs $7.50` — with the price formatted to exactly 2 decimal places.
exercises.starter_code:
price = 7.5
item = "coffee"

# Use an f-string to print: A coffee costs $7.50 (format price to 2 decimal places)
hints: Use {item} to insert the item name
Use {price:.2f} to format the price to 2 decimal places

tests.message: Output contains the correctly formatted price '$7.50'

quiz.question: What does `f"{3 * 4} items"` produce?
quiz.explanation: Python evaluates the expression 3 * 4 = 12 inside the curly braces, then places the result into the string, giving "12 items".

quiz.question: What does the format spec `.2f` do in an f-string?
options: Rounds the number to 2 significant figures
Displays the number with exactly 2 digits before the decimal point
Displays the number with exactly 2 digits after the decimal point
Converts the number to a 2-character string
quiz.explanation: The .2f format spec stands for fixed-point with 2 decimal places. It always shows exactly 2 digits after the decimal point, padding with zeros if needed (e.g. 7.5 becomes 7.50).

completion_message: Well done! F-strings are the cleanest way to build strings in Python. You can now embed variables, compute expressions on the fly, and format numbers beautifully — all inside a single readable string. You will use f-strings constantly in real Python code.
